The Financial Stability Implications of Artificial Intelligence
===============================================================

14 November 2024|[**PDF full text**(583 KB)](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)

> The rapid adoption of AI in finance means that authorities should address information gaps for monitoring, assess the adequacy of current policy frameworks and enhance supervisory and regulatory capabilities.

This report revisits the 2017 FSB report on[AI and machine learning in financial services](https://www.fsb.org/2017/11/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-in-financial-service/) by taking stock of recent advancements, exploring use cases in the financial sector and drivers of adoption, as well as new potential benefits and AI-related financial sector vulnerabilities.

In the past few years, technological advancements and increased computational power have led to an uptake in AI adoption by financial firms and supervisors. AI offers benefits such as increased operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, financial product customisation and advanced analytics. With the advent of generative AI (GenAI) and large language models, the range of use cases has become more diverse.

While AI offers benefits like improved operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, personalised financial products, and advanced data analytics, it may also potentially amplify certain financial sector vulnerabilities. AI-related vulnerabilities that stand out for their potential to increase systemic risk include: (i) third-party dependencies and service provider concentration; (ii) market correlations; (iii) cyber risks; and (iv) model risk, data quality and governance. GenAI also increases the potential for financial fraud and disinformation in financial markets. Misaligned AI systems that are not calibrated to operate within legal, regulatory, and ethical boundaries can also engage in behaviour that harms financial stability. And from a longer-term perspective, AI uptake could also drive changes in market structure, macroeconomic conditions and energy use that could have implications for financial markets and institutions.

While existing regulatory and supervisory frameworks address many of the vulnerabilities associated with AI adoption, more work may be needed to ensure that these frameworks are sufficient. The report calls for national financial authorities and international bodies to enhance monitoring of AI developments, assess whether financial policy frameworks are adequate, and enhance their regulatory and supervisory capabilities including by using AI-powered tools.

Financial institutions worldwide are witnessing a fundamental transformation through the implementation of artificial intelligence. What began as experimental technology has evolved into mission-critical infrastructure that drives competitive advantage and operational excellence.

The numbers speak volumes: [AI adoption in finance](https://www.ema.co/additional-blogs/addition-blogs/understanding-the-role-of-ai-in-revolutionizing-the-fintech-industry) has surged to [85%](https://softwareoasis.com/rise-of-ai-in-financial-services-trends-stats/). This acceleration isn't coincidental; it reflects the measurable value that AI for financial services delivers across multiple operational areas.

This blog will explore the top use cases of AI in financial services, examining how it enhances business operations, reduces risks, and offers unprecedented opportunities for revenue growth.

## TL;DR

## AI for Financial Services: Adoption Trends and Market Landscape

AI has shifted from peripheral technology to a foundational driver of business value in financial services. The industry leads most sectors in terms of AI investment: financial institutions spent [$35 billion](https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidparker/2024/10/03/the-future-of-ai-in-financial-services/) on AI in 2023, with forecasts projecting this will reach [$97 billion](https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidparker/2024/10/03/the-future-of-ai-in-financial-services/) by 2027. This makes financial services one of the most invested industries in AI, spanning banking, insurance, capital markets, and payments.

In many organizations, over a third of core processes have high potential for automation or augmentation with AI, underlining a strong commitment to transforming legacy operations into digital-first models.

### From Efficiency to Growth and Resilience

The initial focus of AI adoption was rapid cost reduction and operational efficiency, which were near-term metrics that delivered tangible ROI. Today, industry leaders are turning their attention to long-term, strategic outcomes.

### Regulatory and Strategic Imperatives

AI is reshaping value chains, market structures, and competitive dynamics. Early and strategic adoption is quickly becoming the differentiator between industry leaders and laggards.

In fact, as per KPMG's Global head of AI and Tax & Legal, Companies that haven't started on the AI journey or are in the very early stages have a common blocker of ROI. However, the research shows that the ROI is there and significant.

## Top Use Cases of AI in Financial Services

![Hero Banner](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.sanity.io%2Fimages%2Fq7d1vb20%2Fproduction%2Fdebbbedb94fe9a8e2aeb646e406ea7dc66a0e2af-1010x611.png&w=2048&q=75)

[Source: https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/sub-issues/digital-finance/artificial-intelligence-in-finance.html](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/sub-issues/digital-finance/artificial-intelligence-in-finance.html)

Artificial intelligence is no longer confined to pilot projects or a single department. It actively reshapes core financial services functions, delivering measurable benefits in efficiency, risk management, and customer engagement.

Below are the top AI use cases decision-makers should prioritize, with key statistics underscoring their impact.

### Customer Service Enhancement

Delivering exceptional customer service is paramount in the financial services industry, and AI is revolutionizing this area. AI-powered solutions like chatbots and virtual assistants are becoming integral to customer interactions.

For example, Bank of America’s AI-driven chatbot, [Erica](https://newsroom.bankofamerica.com/content/newsroom/press-releases/2023/07/bofa-s-erica-surpasses-1-5-billion-client-interactions--totaling.html), has been a key tool in helping clients manage their finances. Since its launch in 2018, Erica has surpassed 1.5 billion interactions, with over 37 million clients using the service. In 2023 alone, clients engaged with Erica more than 333 million times, marking a 35% increase year-over-year. This highlights the scalability and efficiency of AI in handling large volumes of customer interactions.

Moreover, AI systems like Erica can assist human agents by providing real-time data and insights, enabling them to deliver more accurate responses. This approach not only improves customer satisfaction but also reduces operational costs. With AI chatbots expected to handle 80% of customer interactions in banking, they are helping to reduce response times by more than 50%.

### Fraud Detection and Prevention

Fraud remains a significant challenge in financial services, but AI is proving to be a game-changer in combating it. AI’s ability to process vast amounts of data in real-time allows it to detect suspicious activities and fraudulent transactions quickly.

A prime example of this is [HSBC](https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/financial-services/how-hsbc-fights-money-launderers-with-artificial-intelligence), which has implemented AI and machine learning to monitor customer transactions for anomalies. The bank has reported a 50% reduction in false positives within its fraud detection system, improving both operational efficiency and the [customer experience](https://www.ema.co/customer-experience) by minimizing unnecessary alerts and investigations. This not only saves time but also enhances trust with customers, who benefit from more accurate fraud prevention.

AI’s continuous learning capabilities allow systems like HSBC’s to evolve and become more precise over time. As more data flows through, these AI models refine their detection algorithms, ensuring that banks remain one step ahead of increasingly sophisticated cybercriminals.

The success of AI in fraud detection is also reflected in the broader financial services, as NVIDIA's tweet showcases how AI agents are driving improvements in fraud detection, customer service, and overall operational efficiency. Leading institutions such as BlackRock, CapitalOne, and RBC are adopting Agentic AI to enhance productivity, security, and customer experiences.

### Credit Scoring and Risk Management

AI is revolutionizing how financial institutions assess creditworthiness, moving beyond traditional models that rely primarily on historical data. Traditional credit scoring often misses key factors, but AI can analyze a broader range of data, including unstructured data such as transaction histories, social media activity, and more, providing a more comprehensive view of a borrower’s financial behavior.

A prime example of AI’s impact is [Zest AI](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zest-ais-credit-models-proven-to-increase-loan-approvals-for-every-protected-class-302048828.html), a leader in AI-automated underwriting. Over the past four years, Zest AI’s technology has helped lenders increase loan approvals by 49% for Latinos, 41% for Black applicants, 40% for women, 36% for elderly applicants, and 31% for AAPI applicants, all while maintaining consistent risk levels. This demonstrates how AI-driven credit models can promote more inclusive lending practices without compromising risk management. To date, Zest AI has processed over 39 million loan applications, resulting in $250 billion in loans granted.

Zest AI’s models have proven particularly beneficial in increasing access to credit for underrepresented groups, providing a fairer and more equitable approach to lending. The company's AI technology assesses 98% of American adults, enabling lenders to make better, data-driven decisions while significantly boosting loan approval rates.

### AI in Wealth and Asset Management

AI is reshaping wealth management by offering more personalized investment strategies and optimizing portfolio management. AI is transforming wealth management by enhancing portfolio optimization, risk management, and personalized investment strategies.

[BlackRock](https://www.klover.ai/blackrock-ai-strategy-analysis-of-dominance-in-asset-management/), the world’s largest asset manager with $11.6 trillion in assets, leverages AI through its Aladdin platform to drive data-driven investment decisions. Aladdin enables BlackRock to optimize portfolios, manage risk, and identify investment opportunities, leading to record net inflows of $641 billion in 2024.

Aladdin's AI capabilities provide predictive analytics that help fund managers make informed decisions, improving returns and minimizing risks. This platform’s vast data ecosystem allows BlackRock to continually refine AI models, ensuring precision in predicting market trends and adjusting strategies accordingly.

In the trading space, AI powers algorithmic trading. Firms like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley use AI algorithms to execute high-frequency trades, analyzing millions of data points to identify opportunities in real-time. AI trading now accounts for [60-73%](https://www.benzinga.com/general/topics/23/06/32861724/what-percentage-of-stock-trades-are-made-by-bots-and-algorithms) of all equity trades on U.S. exchanges, highlighting AI’s pivotal role in modern trading strategies.

It's the impact of these use cases that is driving more financial institutions, such as banks, to adopt the benefits AI brings to the table. This tweet by AI in Nigeria reflects the same.

## Quantifiable Benefits of AI in Finance for Decision Makers

![Hero Banner](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.sanity.io%2Fimages%2Fq7d1vb20%2Fproduction%2F17ea6b5b9e4de509c5c3dc3c08015bb125c43d25-1900x1080.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

[AI adoption delivers concrete, measurable benefits](https://www.ema.co/additional-blogs/addition-blogs/key-applications-and-benefits-of-ai-in-banking) that go beyond just technology adoption. It’s about creating real efficiencies, cutting costs, and uncovering new avenues for revenue growth. For leaders, these outcomes are proving essential in driving smarter strategies and better results

### Operational Efficiency Gains

AI is significantly improving operational efficiency across the financial services sector. Many financial institutions are reporting notable time savings and enhanced performance due to AI-powered automation. This improvement in efficiency directly translates to reduced costs and optimized use of resources.

For example, document processing, which traditionally required hours of manual review, is now completed in minutes with the help of AI-powered optical character recognition (OCR) and natural language processing (NLP). This allows staff to focus on higher-value tasks while streamlining operations.

### Cost Reduction and ROI

The financial impact of AI implementation extends beyond efficiency gains. Cost savings from AI-led fraud detection will increase to [$10.4](https://http://juniperresearch.com/press/ai-enabled-financial-fraud-detection-spend/) billion globally in 2027, according to Juniper Research projections.

Institutions implementing comprehensive AI strategies report substantial reductions in operational expenses, particularly in areas requiring high-volume data processing or repetitive decision-making tasks.

### Revenue Enhancement

Beyond cost savings, AI in financial services creates new revenue opportunities through improved customer acquisition, enhanced product offerings, and optimized pricing strategies. AI-driven insights enable institutions to identify profitable customer segments, develop targeted marketing campaigns, and launch innovative financial products.

Investment management firms using AI for portfolio optimization consistently deliver superior risk-adjusted returns, attracting additional assets under management and increasing fee income.

US CFOs reveal insights on AI adoption in finance
=================================================

![Image 15](https://www.kyriba.com/_next/image/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fadmin.main-bvxea6i-f4qo6e7ncusey.us-2.platformsh.site%2Fsite-assets%2FresourceImages%2Fregional-cfo-ai-survey.webp&w=1920&q=75)

Artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing financial leadership worldwide, and CFOs in the United States are navigating the unique challenges and opportunities brought by its adoption. Kyriba recently conducted a [global CFO survey](https://www.kyriba.com/resource/cfo-survey-2025-ai-driven-solutions/) of more than 1,000 companies to understand how CFOs and senior financial decision-makers view AI's impact on the financial sector, exploring its strategic benefits as well as the hurdles to integration.

In both the global and US CFO survey results, a key theme that emerges is a trust gap, where concerns about security, privacy, and accuracy create hesitancy toward fully adopting AI-driven solutions. In fact, 78% of US financial leaders identify security and privacy issues as critical challenges. At the same time, the survey highlights financial leaders also recognize AI’s immense potential to transform their roles and drive strategic value.

From caution to innovation, here’s how AI is reshaping the future of finance and evolving the roles of financial leaders in the United States.

Key trends from the US CFO survey
---------------------------------

*   **CFOs highlight security concerns with****AI adoption in finance**: 78% of US CFOs report major concerns about security and privacy risks in AI, with issues like data leaks, accuracy, and regulatory compliance hindering confidence in adoption.

*   **CFOs plan to embrace AI for strategic initiatives**: US CFOs are leveraging AI for high-level tasks such as strategic planning (52%) and investment analysis (48%), shifting its role from operational efficiency to empowering high-level decision-making.

*   **AI drives evolution in financial leadership**: AI is reshaping CFO roles, with 76% highlighting AI literacy as vital for future financial leaders to remain competitive.

_Note: The global survey gathered insights from 1,000 CFOs at companies generating $100M+ in revenue, with 250 respondents from the United States. Many survey questions prompted respondents to select more than one answer. Due to rounding, percentages may not always appear to add up to 100%._

Why US CFOs are cautious about AI adoption in finance
-----------------------------------------------------

US CFOs express significant concerns about adopting and using AI in finance, with trust issues rooted in security, privacy, and ethical challenges. The distrust in AI-based tools is understandable, with AI errors carrying the potential for financial losses, regulatory breaches, mishandling of sensitive data, and mismanagement of liquidity.

When asked to identify the biggest challenge related to adopting AI technologies in their finance departments, it’s clear that building trust in AI systems is a significant hurdle to adoption. US CFOs indicate security and ethics as their number one concern (31%), followed by allocation of resources (21%), and lack of internal knowledge (17%).

When considering AI use in their finance operations, over half (54%) of US CFOs highlight security/leaking of confidential information as the top concern, followed by accuracy (46%) and regulatory compliance (42%). While the promise of AI is undeniable, these findings demonstrate a critical need for AI systems built with security, transparency, and accountability at their core.

![Image 16: Top US CFO concerns about AI use in finance](https://admin.main-bvxea6i-f4qo6e7ncusey.us-2.platformsh.site/site-assets/resourceImages/Top-US-CFO-concerns-about-AI-use-in-finance.webp)
**Top US CFO concerns about AI use in finance**

This trust gap extends to broader organizational risks, with 78% of US financial leaders identifying security and privacy issues as critical external challenges, alongside inflation/cost of living (80%), market volatility (75%), and interest rates (74%).

These concerns show that US CFOs are cautious about AI-specific risks while also viewing security as a broader organizational challenge. The consequences of this distrust are profound. If security and privacy concerns remain unaddressed, organizations may find it challenging to unlock AI's full potential.

Establishing trust through comprehensive security measures is crucial to bridging this gap. By adopting robust security protocols, CFOs can embrace AI with confidence, leveraging it to foster innovation, improve decision-making, and effectively manage the complexities of modern finance. Without these measures, the trust gap will persist, hindering AI adoption.

“AI-focused skills will empower finance professionals to confidently work with AI technologies and bridge the trust gap by ensuring decisions made by AI systems are transparent and understandable. … By combining human expertise with AI's analytical capabilities, organizations can make more informed decisions.”

_-Morné Rossouw, Chief AI Officer, Kyriba_

_Explore the full interview in our [global CFO survey](https://www.kyriba.com/resource/cfo-survey-2025-ai-driven-solutions/) for more insights._

AI in action: US CFOs seize opportunities beyond the gap
--------------------------------------------------------

While the challenges surrounding AI adoption in finance are significant, its potential to drive financial resilience and innovation is undeniable. Once CFOs can trust AI, they can unlock many opportunities and benefits, such as greater operational efficiency, more accurate financial insights, and extra time to focus on strategic initiatives.

Despite the significant trust gap, US CFOs are embracing AI’s potential:

*   56% are leveraging AI in the majority of their financial decision-making processes, outpacing the UK (50%), France (36%), and Japan (25%).

*   94% feel prepared to adopt AI technologies in treasury and finance operations.

*   98% prioritize AI integration in their business operations.

For US organizations, the timeline to put AI in action is also more accelerated than global trends. Nearly 60% of US CFOs plan to integrate AI into their treasury and finance operations within the next 12 months, compared to just 36% of CFOs worldwide. These trends indicate that US CFOs are in a strong position–as early adopters of AI are poised to set the standard for financial leadership, compelling others to follow suit.

By leveraging AI, businesses gain the agility needed to address external factors such as market volatility, inflation, tariff impacts, and geopolitical risks. And similar to their global counterparts, 56% of US CFOs are already using AI as the top tactic to navigate these external factors.

The US CFO survey results reveal increasing enthusiasm to leverage AI for improvements and innovations in financial operations. When asked what excites them about the potential of using AI in finance operations, US CFOs highlight:

![Image 17: What excites US CFOs about AI’s potential in finance](https://admin.main-bvxea6i-f4qo6e7ncusey.us-2.platformsh.site/site-assets/resourceImages/What-excites-US-CFOs-about-AI%E2%80%99s-potential-in-finance.webp)
**What excites US CFOs about AI’s potential in finance**

As organizations evolve to embrace AI-based tools, they stand to increasingly benefit from enhanced efficiency, accuracy, and compliance, while driving cost-effectiveness and revenue growth in financial operations.

The US survey findings send a clear signal that CFOs see the value of leveraging AI to enhance decision-making and drive strategic growth. When asked how their organizations plan to use AI for treasury operations throughout 2025 and into 2026, US CFOs cite:

![Image 18: How US CFOs plan to use AI for treasury operations](https://admin.main-bvxea6i-f4qo6e7ncusey.us-2.platformsh.site/site-assets/resourceImages/How-US-CFOs-plan-to-use-AI-for-treasury-operations.webp)
**How US CFOs plan to use AI for treasury operations**

The survey results indicate a shift in how CFOs perceive AI’s potential in finance. Rather than viewing it solely as a tool for basic automation and operational efficiency, the majority now see it as a driver for high-level strategic transformation. For organizations slowly adopting AI, there is a clear risk of falling behind.

A patchwork of legislation keeps US CFOs on their toes
------------------------------------------------------

AI legislation in the United States is evolving, but remains fragmented. Currently, there are no comprehensive federal regulations or legislation governing artificial intelligence. While initiatives like the [National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020](https://www.congress.gov/bill/116th-congress/house-bill/6216) have provided a strategic framework, federal action has primarily relied on adapting existing regulations to AI's unique challenges. Recent [executive orders](https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/01/removing-barriers-to-american-leadership-in-artificial-intelligence/) reflect shifting priorities, promoting US leadership in AI innovation.

At the state level, a surge of activity is shaping the regulatory landscape. Nearly 100 AI-related bills became law across the nation in 2024, with states like Colorado and California leading the charge. [Colorado’s AI Act](https://leg.colorado.gov/sites/default/files/2024a_205_signed.pdf), for example, is the first comprehensive state law targeting high-risk AI systems, prioritizing transparency, fairness, and consumer protection. California has passed several [noteworthy laws](https://www.whitecase.com/insight-alert/raft-california-ai-legislation-adds-growing-patchwork-us-regulation), including mandates on AI-generated content disclosures and controls against deepfake deception. For CFOs, these state-level efforts could represent a patchwork regulatory framework in the absence of federal oversight, underscoring the importance of staying informed and compliant as they consider AI integration.

AI drives evolution in US CFO leadership
----------------------------------------

An important takeaway from the survey is AI’s role in reshaping CFO responsibilities. From automating repetitive tasks to enabling data-driven decision-making, AI is redefining what it means to be a CFO.

“Traditionally focused on compliance and reporting, CFOs are now becoming strategic advisors. AI reduces transactional tasks, enabling us to interpret predictive insights and guide long-term strategies. The role is evolving from number-crunching to connecting data with broader organizational goals.”

-Adam Drew, CFO, Kyriba

_Explore the full interview with Adam Drew in our [global CFO survey](https://www.kyriba.com/resource/cfo-survey-2025-ai-driven-solutions/) for more insights._

Over two-thirds (72%) of US financial leaders believe AI will drive the most significant transformation in their roles over the next five years, more so than workforce shifts (60%) and C-suite succession (50%). These findings signal a growing recognition among financial leaders that AI will be a pivotal force in shaping the future of financial leadership, driving innovation and redefining traditional roles to meet the demands of a rapidly changing economic environment.

US CFOs are also anticipating a future where AI literacy becomes a core competency. Three-fourths (76%) indicate that future CFOs and treasurers will need strong competencies in AI and technology to maintain a competitive advantage, ranking higher than learning about risk management (59%) and the role of a CEO (54%). These findings show that US financial leaders are prioritizing AI and technology skills as essential for staying competitive, highlighting a shift toward a more tech-savvy and strategically focused approach to financial leadership.

US CFO AI adoption trends advance financial leadership
------------------------------------------------------

US CFO AI adoption trends mark a defining moment for financial leadership, with artificial intelligence emerging as both a catalyst for transformation and a challenge to overcome. The trust gap, driven by concerns about security, privacy, and accuracy, underscores a critical opportunity for organizations to differentiate themselves. By fostering trust through transparency, stronger data governance, and collaboration, CFOs can lead their organizations toward fully realizing AI’s transformative power, enhancing their roles as strategic drivers of innovation and growth.

AI is far more than a tool for efficiency; it is redefining the way financial leaders approach decision-making, risk management, and long-term strategy. With a majority of US CFOs identifying AI literacy as pivotal to staying competitive, the future of finance depends on leaders who blend technological proficiency with visionary expertise. By embedding AI into strategic priorities like planning and forecasting, today’s CFOs can position their organizations to thrive in an era of rapid change and complexity.

# Top 10 AI Use Cases for Finance Operations

From spend analysis to faster close cycles, AI is helping finance leaders find new efficiencies, reduce risk, and reallocate resources for strategic growth. Learn the major benefits that your business could gain from AI.

From spend analysis to faster close cycles, AI is helping finance leaders find new efficiencies, reduce risk, and reallocate resources for strategic growth. Learn the major benefits that your business could gain from AI.

![](https://media.mktg.workday.com/is/image/workday/sydney-scott:Square?wid=150&hei=150)
![Two people smiling down looking at tablet](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAEAAAABCAQAAAC1HAwCAAAAC0lEQVR42mP8Xw8AAoMBgDTD2qgAAAAASUVORK5CYII=)

In this article we discuss:

Today’s finance teams face pressure to maintain low operating costs while also processing higher transaction volumes and acting with agility. But outdated legacy systems often get in the way, creating bottlenecks that hinder growth.

Teams need a solution that can scale with their operations while staying precise, accurate, and insightful. AI is bridging the gap, offering more sophisticated financial planning and insight while still driving efficiencies across the finance function.

For high-level leaders, a positive outlook on artificial intelligence (AI) is nearly unanimous—[98% of CEOs](https://www.workday.com/en-au/artificial-intelligence/research/ai-in-business.html) say their business would immediately benefit from AI implementation. Finance in particular is leading the way on use cases, integration, and showing ROI—[77% of finance AI pioneers](https://forms.workday.com/en-au/reports/global-cfo-ai-indicator-preview/form.html) have already embraced it in their day to day operations.

Still, there’s a definite gap between those who see AI’s potential value and those actually integrating it successfully. Knowing the strongest use casesAI in finance to drive cost savings and enhance team performance is key to keeping your business competitive in the future.

By anchoring AI implementation to tangible goals, finance teams can demonstrate ROI and secure support for broader adoption.

## What Is AI in Finance?

**AI in finance** refers to the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning (ML) to analyze data, automate processes, and generate insights for the finance function. Finance departments handle large amounts of data governed by strict rules—conditions where AI excels. Leveraging ML algorithms and advanced analytics, AI  intelligently automates repetitive tasks, surfacing insights hidden in volumes of transactions.

As AI models refine their predictions over time, finance teams gain an adaptive partner that strengthens their compliance, accuracy, and decision speed without supplanting human oversight. For financial services companies, it also provides an important way to provide more personalized customer experiences. Key capabilities of [AI in finance](https://www.workday.com/en-au/topics/ai/ai-in-finance.html) include:

**Adaptive learning:** Models continuously improve predictions by analyzing vast amounts of data and patterns.

**Intelligent automation:** Routine tasks like data extraction and reconciliation run with minimal oversight.

**Predictive insights:** Advanced forecasting tools anticipate cash flow needs and risk exposures before they arise.

**Real-time monitoring:** Continuous anomaly detection flags irregularities instantly, enabling prompt intervention and risk management.

**Scalable integration:** AI layers integrate with existing ERP and finance systems, preserving core workflows while boosting capability.

Leaders in finance are moving beyond small AI experiments to truly integrate AI across their entire operations. This shift is leading to real results. In fact, the [Workday CFO AI Indicator Report](https://forms.workday.com/en-au/reports/global-cfo-ai-indicator-preview/form.html) found that finance leaders have high confidence in AI’s ability to deliver business benefits to their organizations, with increased revenue/profits and lower costs both in the top five benefits listed.

## 10 Best Use Cases for AI in Finance

When implemented wisely, AI in finance turns tricky hidden inefficiencies into measurable savings. This means teams can operate effectively and seamlessly. Below are ten key areas where AI-powered tools provide the strongest utility and free up time to focus on more strategic, value-driving work.

### 1. Automated Transaction Capture

Finance teams often spend hours manually entering invoice data. AI-driven OCR algorithms and [natural language processing (NLP)](https://www.workday.com/en-us/topics/ai/natural-language-processing.html) models ingest unstructured documents, extract line-items, and auto-populate financial systems. By eliminating manual input and adapting to new document formats, companies reduce costly error-related rework.

### 2. Intelligent Exception Handling

Traditional automation can point out errors, but often misses the big picture. AI applications analyse transaction patterns to understand what’s normal and what’s a red flag, with humans stepping in to review anomalies. This smart approach means less time spent reviewing, faster-problem solving, and a significant cut in overall operational costs.

### 3. Predictive Cash Flow Management

Accurate forecasting prevents costly overdrafts and idle cash. AI models ingest historical payments, seasonality, and market indicators to predict liquidity needs. By suggesting optimal payment schedules and collection priorities, finance functions lower borrowing costs and improve interest income.

### 4. Dynamic Fraud Detection

Static rule-based systems can sometimes miss evolving threats. On the other hand, machine learning models continuously learn from transaction data to detect subtle fraud patterns in real time. Early alerts can prevent big financial losses from credit card fraud or money laundering, and save money on investigations by handling them quickly.

### 5. Accelerated Close Processes

Month-end reconciliations can be a drag for financial institutions. AI-powered systems help by comparing ledgers, suggesting journal entries, and learning to spot and fix mistakes over time. This speeds up close cycles, reduces external audit fees, and accelerates stakeholder reporting.

### 6. Proactive Compliance Monitoring

Regulatory requirements change rapidly across different regions. Using NLP, AI-driven compliance tools parse policy updates and monitor transactions for adherence, generating audit-ready reports automatically. Organizations save on legal reviews and mitigate the risk of costly fines.

### 7. Strategic Spend Insights

AI analytics platforms categorize expenditures and highlight maverick buying by learning typical vendor behaviors. Finance leaders gain visibility into overspend and contract deviations, enabling targeted negotiations and cost reductions.

### 8. Optimized Procurement Planning

Inventory misalignment ties up capital. Predictive AI models analyze demand trends and supplier performance to recommend reorder points and preferred vendors. This helps reduce carrying costs and emergency replenishment fees.

### 9. Workflow Optimization

Process-mining applications help visualize every step, identifying bottlenecks and redundant steps. AI algorithms offer suggestions for automating workflows, making everything faster and cutting down expenses. Increasingly, finance AI agents are even handling workflows autonomously so teams can stay focused on higher-level work.

### 10. Workforce Effectiveness

Automating repetitive tasks by leveraging AI allows finance professionals to redirect more time, energy, and expertise to areas like financial analysis and strategic planning. Redirecting talent toward high-value activities enhances team productivity and maximizes ROI.

Seventy-seven percent of finance AI pioneers have already embraced AI in their day-to-day operations.

## How to Use AI in Finance: A 5-Step Roadmap

Rolling out AI for your finance team demands a targeted and metrics-driven approach. Finance leaders should prioritize high-impact processes, establish clear KPIs around expense reduction, and build a framework that balances quick wins with sustainable scalability.

By anchoring each phase of implementation to tangible goals, teams can demonstrate ROI early and secure executive support for broader adoption.These steps can help you adopt AI into your finance function smoothly.

### 1. Prioritize High-Impact Use Cases

Start by looking at your finance tasks to find where repetitive work and fixing mistakes eat up costs and time. Talk with AP clerks, accountants, and controllers to understand daily bottlenecks and common exception triggers. Document how long tasks take and how often people have to step in. This will help you select one or two processes, like approving invoices or reconciling accounts, where machine learning algorithms can quickly make an impact.

### 2. Establish Unified Data Platform

Work with IT to consolidate AP, AR, general ledger, and other relevant feeds into a centralized data repository. Standardize data formats and implement simple validation checks—such as mandatory fields and range validations—to catch anomalies early. Set up a basic dashboard tracking key metrics (e.g., processing time, exception rates) to ensure data quality remains high and supports reliable AI training and ongoing monitoring.

### 3. Deploy AI Models

Choose an AI solution that clearly matches what you want to achieve. This could be AI that helps pull information from documents or generative AI that creates financial reports from that data. Collaborate with your vendor or internal data science team to feed the model historical transaction data to help it learn about specific document styles and rules. Be sure to connect the AI to existing finance systems to ensure it pulls data and sends alerts seamlessly.

### 4. Validate Savings

Before full rollout, measure baseline metrics: average processing time, volume of exceptions, and manual reconciliation hours. Run the AI solution in parallel with current workflows (known as shadow mode) for a short pilot period. Compare AI-driven results to your baseline to quantify time saved and reduction in manual errors. Use any discrepancies to refine model parameters and exception-handling rules before switching over.

### 5. Scale Continuous Optimization

Establish a quarterly review cadence with stakeholders from finance, IT, and operations to assess AI performance and process metrics. Retrain models on fresh data, adjust automation thresholds as transaction patterns evolve, and update validation rules. Apply lessons learned to new finance processes—such as tax compliance or treasury operations—extending cost savings step by step while maintaining governance and transparency.

## Take the Next Steps

AI is proving to be a catalyst for real transformation. When AI is built into your core finance processes, it constantly reveals new ways to improve efficiency. Leaders gain precise knowledge to anticipate what’s next, handle risks, and use resources wisely. This in turn empowers teams to go beyond routine tasks and focus instead on strategic analysis that fuels informed decisions.

You don’t have to overhaul every aspect of your financial processes at once. The journey to AI in finance begins with a targeted project where AI's ability to reduce costs can be clearly seen and measured right away. These early successes create confidence and inspire wider use, turning AI from a simple experiment into a vital part of your strategic planning. Over time, as each new area of finance is thoughtfully integrated, AI becomes the foundation for a competitive edge that drives lasting value.
